elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we shall compare two metrics in terms of `orderly dependence’, a notion developed in exponential vector space in the article `Basis and Dimension of Exponential Vector Space' by Jayeeta Saha and Sandip Jana in Transactions of A.
elements.sent-2411-003: Exponential vector space (in short `evs') is a partially ordered space associated with a commutative semigroup structure and a compatible scalar multiplication.
elements.sent-2411-004: In the present paper we shall show that the collection $ \mathcal{D(\mathbf X) $ of all metrics on a non-empty set $ \mathbf X $, together with the constant function zero `$ O $', forms a topological exponential vector space.}
elements.sent-2411-005: We shall discuss the orderly dependence of two metrics through our findings of a basis of $ \mathcal{D(\mathbf X)}\smallsetminus\{O\} $ in different scenario.
elements.sent-2411-006: We shall characterise `orderly independence' of two elements of a topological evs in terms of the `comparing function', another mechanism developed in topological exponential vector space, which can measure the degree of comparability of two elements of an evs.
elements.sent-2411-007: Finally, we shall discuss existence of orderly independent norms on a linear space.
elements.sent-2411-008: For an infinite dimensional linear space we shall construct a large number of orderly independent norms depending on the dimension of the linear space.
elements.sent-2411-009: Orderly independent norms are precisely those which are `totally non-equivalent', in the sense that they produce incomparable topologies.
elements.sent-2411-011: Key words : Exponential vector space, orderly independence, basis and dimension of evs, comparing function, totally non-equivalent norm.
elements.sent-2411-012: Two metrics on a set are said to be equivalent if they produce the same topology.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the present paper we shall compare two metrics in terms of basis of exponential vector space.
elements.sent-2411-014: The study of exponential vector space was initiated by S.. Ganguly, S.
elements.sent-2411-016: Jana in the paper \cite{C(X) with the name `quasi vector space'.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Later the term `exponential vector space' was coined by Priti Sharma and Sandip Jana in \cite{evs .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Let us first present the definition of exponential vector space.
elements.sent-2411-019: If the operations and the partial order satisfy the following axioms then $(X,+,\cdot,\leq)$ is called an exponential vector space (in short evs) over $K$ .
elements.sent-2411-020: This vector space $X_0$ is called the `primitive space' or `zero space' of $X$ and the elements of $X_0$ are called the `primitive elements' [\cite{evs ].}
elements.sent-2411-021: Thus every evs contains a vector space and conversely, given any vector space $V$, an evs $X$ can be constructed such that $V$ is isomorphic to $X_0$ \cite{evs .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this sense,``exponential vector space'' can be considered as an algebraic ordered extension of vector space.
elements.sent-2411-023: The axiom $ A_3 $(iii) expresses very rapid growth of the non-primitive elements, since $x\leq \frac {1 {2 x +\frac{1 {2 x$, $\forall x \not \in \thinspace X_0$; whereas axiom $ A_6 $ demonstrates `positivity' of all elements with respect to primitive elements.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: This justifies the nomenclature `exponential vector space'.
elements.sent-2411-025: Notation: For any set $ A\subseteq X,\ X $ being an evs, we shall use the following notations: \centerline{$\uparrow A:=\{x\in X: x\geq a\text{ for some a\in A\}$ and $\downarrow A:=\{x\in X: x\leq a\text{ for some a\in A\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The following concepts will be useful in the sequel.
elements.sent-2411-027: Moreover, the closedness of the partial order `$\leq$' in a topological exponential vector space $X$ readily implies (in view of Theorem \ref{t:partcl ) that $X$ is Hausdorff and hence $X_0$ becomes a Hausdorff topological vector space.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Define operations and partial order on $X$ as follows : for $(r,a),(s,b) \in X $ and $\alpha \in \mathbb K $, (i) $(r,a)+(s,b) := (r+s,a+b)$ (ii) $\alpha (r,a) := (\lvert\alpha\rvert r,\alpha a)$ (iii) $(r,a)\leq (s,b)$ iff $r\leq s$ and $a = b$ Then [$0,\infty$)$\times V$ becomes an exponential vector space with the primitive space \{$0$\}$\times V$ which is clearly isomorphic to $V$.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this example, if we consider $V$ as a Hausdorff topological vector space, then [$0,\infty$)$\times V$ becomes a topological exponential vector space with respect to the product topology, where [$0,\infty$) is equipped with the subspace topology inherited from the real line $\mathbb R$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Instead of $V$, if we take the trivial vector space $ \{\theta\} $ in the above example, then the resulting topological evs is [$0,\infty$)$\times\{ \theta\}$ which can be clearly identified with the half ray [$0,\infty$) of the real line.
elements.sent-2411-031: Thus, [$0,\infty$) forms a topological evs over the field $\mathbb K$.\qed \end{Ex \begin{Ex {\cite{C(X) Let $\mathscr{C (\X)$ denote the topological hyperspace consisting of all non-empty compact subsets of a Hausdorff topological vector space $\X$ over the field $\K$ of real or complex numbers.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Define addition, scalar multiplication and partial order on $\mathscr{C (\X)$ as follows: (i) For $A,B\in\mathscr{C (\X)$, $A+B:=$ $\big\{a+b:$ $a\in A$, $b\in B\big\}$ (ii) For $A\in\mathscr{C (\X)$ and $\alpha\in\K$, $\alpha A:=$ $\big\{\alpha a:$ $a\in A\big\}$ (iii) For $A,B\in\mathscr{C (\X)$, $A\leq B\iff$ $A\subseteq B$ Then $\mathscr{C (\X)$ becomes an evs over the field $\K$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-050: An evs $X$ over $\K$ is said to be a balanced evs if each element of $X$ is a balanced element.

elements.sent-2411-052: An evs $X$ over $\K$ is said to be homogeneous if each element of $X$ is a homogeneous element.

elements.sent-2411-149: We shall now compare two metrics or two norms in the following sections using the theory of exponential vector space.
elements.section-2411-004: Comparability of metrics on a non-empty set
elements.sent-2411-150: Two metrics on a non-empty set can be compared in several ways.
elements.sent-2411-151: The most common is by comparing the topologies they induce.
elements.sent-2411-152: In this respect two metrics are called equivalent if they produce the same topology.
elements.sent-2411-153: Also we may compare two metrics by comparing which topology, induced by them, is finer.
elements.sent-2411-154: Another one is by comparing their completeness property.
elements.sent-2411-155: In this section we shall compare two metrics in terms of `orderly dependence', the order being the partial order of the exponential vector space structure of $ \mathcal{D(\X) $.}
elements.sent-2411-156: This notion was introduced in the article `Basis and Dimension of Exponential Vector Space' \cite{basis .}
elements.sent-2411-157: This mode of comparison also has some connection with the above mentioned `completeness' and `equivalence'.
elements.sent-2411-158: Let us first present some preliminary definitions and results related to basis and dimension of exponential vector space.
elements.sent-2411-159: Define \centerline{$L(x):=\big\{z\in X: z\geq \alpha x + p,\alpha\in\K^{* ,p\in X_0\big\},\text{ where }\K^{*}\equiv \K\smallsetminus\{0\}.$} These sets $L(x)$ for different $x\in X\smallsetminus X_0$ are named as testing sets of $X$.\index{Testing set \end{Def \begin{Prop {\em\cite{basis (i) $\forall\, x\in X\smallsetminus X_{0}$, $x\in L(x)$ and $\uparrow L(x)=L(x)$, (ii) $x\leq y\,(x,y\in X\smallsetminus X_{0 )\implies L(x)\supseteq L(y)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: So generator always exists for $X\smallsetminus X_{0}$.
elements.sent-2411-161: Any superset of a generator of $X\smallsetminus X_{0}$ is also a generator of $X\smallsetminus X_{0}$.
elements.sent-2411-162: A subset $ B $ of $ X\smallsetminus X_0 $ is said to be orderly independent if any two members $ x, y\in B $ are orderly independent.
elements.sent-2411-163: Thus two orderly independent elements of $ X $ cannot be comparable with respect to the partial order.
elements.sent-2411-164: Then we shall represent dimension of the evs $X$ as $\text{ dim (X):=[\text{dim (X\smallsetminus X_0):\text{dim X_0]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: If $X_0=\{\theta\}$, then dimension of $X_0$ will be taken as $0$, since then $X_0$ has no basis [as vector space].
elements.sent-2411-166: Then $\downarrow x\smallsetminus X_{0}\subseteq L(x)$, for each $x\in B$.
elements.sent-2411-167: The set $Q(X)$ is called feasible set of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-168: If for an evs $ X $, $Q(X)=\emptyset$, then $X\smallsetminus X_{0}$ does not have a basis.
elements.sent-2411-169: Also for an evs $X$, $Q(X)\not=\emptyset$ does not necessarily imply that $X\smallsetminus X_{0}$ possesses a basis.
elements.sent-2411-170: This necessitates finding of basis of $\mathcal{D (\mathbf{X )\smallsetminus\{O\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: So it becomes indispensable to determine the feasible set $ Q\big(\mathcal D(\mathbf X)\big) $.

elements.sent-2411-173: Further we use symbol $\rho_{_u}$ for denoting usual metric on $\R$.

elements.section-2411-005: Comparability of metrics using comparing function
elements.sent-2411-338: In this section we shall compare two metrics using `comparing function'.
elements.sent-2411-339: This is a notion used in topological exponential vector space to measure the degree of comparability of two elements.
elements.sent-2411-340: Using this concept we shall characterise orderly independence of two elements in a topological exponential vector space.
elements.sent-2411-341: We shall compute the comparing function in $ \mathcal{D(\X) $ and use it to determine orderly dependency of some standard metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-342: For $x,y\in X$, we define the comparing spectrum of $y$ relative to $x$ by $\sigma_{x (y):=\big\{\lambda\in\K:$ $\lambda x\leq y\big\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-343: Since $X$ is zero primitive, $y\geq\theta$ for all $y\in X$ and hence $0\in\sigma_x(y)$ so that it is nonempty.
elements.sent-2411-344: For any $x\,(\neq\theta)\in X$, the comparing function relative to $x$, denoted by $C_{x}$, is defined as \centerline{$C_{x (y):=\underset{\lambda\in\sigma_{x (y) {\sup |\lambda|$, for all $y\in X$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-345: By Proposition \ref{p:specbdd , $C_x$ is well-defined and $C_x(y)\in[0,\infty), \forall\,y\in X,\forall\,x\neq\theta$.}
elements.sent-2411-346: For any non-zero homogeneous element $x\in X$ and any $y\in X$, $C_x(y)x\leq y$.
elements.sent-2411-347: If $ y\in L(x) $ then $\exists \,\alpha\in\K^* $ such that $ y\geq\alpha x $ which implies $ C_x(y)\geq|\alpha|>0 $.
elements.sent-2411-348: Conversely, if $ C_x(y)>0 $ then by Proposition \ref{p:leq , $ C_x(y)x\leq y $ and hence $ y\in L(x) $.}
elements.sent-2411-349: This immediately implies the following Theorem.

elements.section-2411-006: Comparability of norms on a linear space
elements.sent-2411-369: We shall explore, in this section, the techniques developed in the previous sections to investigate the comparability of two norms on a linear space.
elements.sent-2411-370: Here comparability of two norms is judged by the concept of orderly dependence; so to explore comparability of norms we need to investigate some basis of $ \norm\smallsetminus\{O\} $ (Example \ref{e:spofnorms ).}
elements.sent-2411-371: Equivalence of any two norms on a finite dimensional linear space $ \X $ ensures the existence of a basis in $ \norm\smallsetminus\{O\} $.
elements.sent-2411-372: We shall prove that in an infinite dimensional linear space, a large number of orderly independent norms can be constructed depending on the dimension of the linear space.
